title: Consulting Firm
summary: Students operate as professional consultants who diagnose a client problem and deliver an evidence-based, implementation-ready recommendation.
scenario: A neighborhood entertainment venue has strong customer reviews but declining attendance and weak weekday revenue.

title: Boardroom Meeting
summary: Students serve as executives and board members who evaluate a major proposal, question assumptions, manage competing interests, and vote on a final course of action.
scenario: A regional retailer is considering closing two stores and investing the savings in e-commerce and same-day delivery.

title: Crisis Management Team
summary: Students form a cross-functional response team that stabilizes a business crisis, communicates with stakeholders, and builds a recovery plan as new information arrives.
scenario: A beverage company receives reports that several bottles may contain an undeclared allergen while social media posts are spreading rapidly.

title: Product Launch Simulation
summary: Students coordinate customer research, product design, pricing, distribution, promotion, operations, and risk to build and defend a complete launch plan.
scenario: A snack company is launching a high-protein product for high school and college athletes with a fixed first-quarter budget.

title: Executive Decision Matrix
summary: Students act as an executive team that defines decision criteria, weighs competing options, tests assumptions, and recommends a defensible course of action.
scenario: A growing company must choose between opening a new location, improving its current facility, expanding online sales, or developing a new service.

title: Business Negotiation
summary: Students prepare interests, priorities, alternatives, and tradeable terms before negotiating an agreement that creates value and protects essential needs.
scenario: A school event organizer negotiates a sponsorship agreement with a local company involving cash, promotion, exclusivity, tickets, and community visibility.

title: Company Turnaround
summary: Students take control of a struggling organization, diagnose the causes of decline, choose difficult priorities, and present a measurable recovery plan.
scenario: A once-popular clothing retailer has declining store traffic, excess inventory, high rent, weak online sales, and an unclear brand.

title: Hiring Committee
summary: Students define job criteria, evaluate candidate evidence, conduct structured interviews, manage bias, and recommend the strongest hire.
scenario: A growing recreation company is hiring a marketing coordinator who must manage social media, partnerships, events, data, and customer communication.

title: Case Study Carousel
summary: Students rotate through authentic business cases and build on prior teams' analyses.
scenario: Teams rotate through several business cases and improve the recommendation at each stop.

title: Silent Discussion
summary: Students hold a complete academic discussion in writing, responding to prompts and one another before any whole-class conversation begins.
scenario: Students evaluate whether a company should raise prices after costs increase, knowing the decision may affect customers, employees, and profit.

title: Socratic Seminar for Business
summary: Students use a shared text, case, dataset, or business decision to lead a disciplined discussion built on evidence, questioning, listening, and revision.
scenario: Students discuss whether a profitable company should close an underperforming location that remains important to the local community.

title: Chalk Talk
summary: Students build a visible web of ideas, questions, evidence, and connections around a business concept without requiring immediate oral participation.
scenario: Students map why a well-known retail brand may be losing relevance with younger customers.

title: Structured Academic Controversy
summary: Students investigate competing positions, argue both sides, listen for strongest evidence, and then build a reasoned conclusion rather than trying to win a debate.
scenario: A company must decide whether to replace part of its workforce with automation that may lower costs but disrupt employees and the community.

title: Decision Matrix
summary: Students define criteria, assign importance, score alternatives with evidence, and test whether a recommendation remains strong when assumptions change.
scenario: A student-run business must choose the best product to launch from four ideas with different costs, demand, risks, and operational requirements.

title: Affinity Mapping
summary: Students sort a large set of observations, evidence, ideas, or customer comments into meaningful clusters and use the emerging patterns to identify priorities.
scenario: Students analyze 40 customer comments about a school store to identify the most important experience, product, price, and service issues.

title: Think-Pair-Share
summary: Build thinking time into discussion so every student has something worth saying.
scenario: Should Costco raise the price of its $1.50 hot dog combo?

title: Gallery Walk
summary: Move students through evidence, examples, or decisions and make them respond to prior thinking.
scenario: Compare six advertisements by target audience, message, appeal, evidence, and likely weakness.

title: Four Corners
summary: Make students commit to a position, defend it, and reconsider after hearing stronger evidence.
scenario: A local coffee shop faces higher labor and ingredient costs. Should it raise prices, reduce portions, cut hours, or accept lower profit?

title: Jigsaw
summary: Turn students into specialists who must teach one piece of a larger business problem.
scenario: Expert groups study pricing, promotion, distribution, customer experience, and competition before advising a struggling retailer.

title: Fishbowl
summary: Use an inner discussion and outer observation circle to strengthen speaking and listening.
scenario: Should a company use customer data to personalize prices?

title: Station Rotation
summary: Break a long lesson into focused stations for practice, feedback, technology, and teacher support.
scenario: Rotate through budgeting, credit, insurance, investing, and consumer-protection mini-cases.

title: Speed Networking
summary: Use repeated short conversations to build fluency, networking skills, and rapid review.
scenario: Students repeatedly explain a value proposition to a new partner, improving it after every round.

title: Round Robin
summary: Guarantee each group member contributes before anyone speaks twice.
scenario: Teams identify risks in a new food-truck launch, with every student contributing one distinct risk.

title: Shark Tank
summary: Students pitch a business idea or strategic recommendation, defend assumptions, and respond to structured investor questions.
scenario: Pitch a realistic product improvement that solves a school or community problem.

title: Case Study Teams
summary: Students operate as management teams to diagnose a case, evaluate evidence and alternatives, and defend an implementation-ready recommendation.
scenario: A movie theater must choose between premium seating, lower prices, special events, or membership plans.

title: TED-Style Business Talk
summary: Students deliver a focused, idea-driven business talk that explains one important insight through evidence, story, and clear audience takeaways.
scenario: Students explain one lesson a struggling brand should learn about rebuilding customer trust.

title: Trade Show Exhibition
summary: Student teams design an interactive exhibit and speak repeatedly with visitors as they explain, demonstrate, and defend a business idea.
scenario: Teams create booths for new products designed to solve a problem faced by high school students.

title: Executive Briefing
summary: Students deliver a concise, decision-focused briefing that gives leaders the essential evidence, recommendation, risk, and next action without unnecessary detail.
scenario: Students brief school leaders on whether the school store should change its product mix and operating hours.

title: Sales Presentation
summary: Students diagnose a customer's needs, present a relevant value proposition, respond to objections, and ask for a realistic next step.
scenario: Students sell a workplace productivity service to a small business with limited money and concerns about employee adoption.

title: Product Demonstration
summary: Students show how a product or service works, connect features to customer value, anticipate usage questions, and demonstrate the experience clearly for a real audience.
scenario: Students demonstrate a new school-focused product designed to save time, improve organization, or solve a recurring student problem.

title: Press Conference
summary: Students represent an organization facing public questions, deliver a clear opening statement, answer reporters with evidence, and manage difficult or unexpected issues professionally.
scenario: Students represent a company responding to concerns about a delayed product, customer complaints, and unclear public communication.

title: Board Presentation
summary: Students present a strategic recommendation to a board, justify it with evidence, address financial and stakeholder consequences, and respond to governance-level questions.
scenario: Students ask a fictional board to approve a major expansion, new product line, or operational investment.

title: Investor Pitch
summary: Students pitch a business opportunity to investors by explaining the problem, customer, solution, market, business model, evidence, financial logic, and funding request.
scenario: Students pitch a startup concept that solves a recurring problem for students, families, local businesses, or community organizations.

title: Exit Ticket
summary: Capture what students learned, misunderstood, or can apply before they leave.
scenario: Recommend one pricing strategy for the day's company and support it with one reason.

title: 3-2-1 Reflection
summary: Use a predictable reflection frame to surface learning, questions, and next steps.
scenario: Three ideas learned, two real-world connections, and one remaining question.

title: Retrieval Grid
summary: Students retrieve key ideas from memory across a structured grid, compare answers, correct misconceptions, and strengthen connections between concepts before an assessment.
scenario: Students complete a grid that mixes vocabulary, examples, calculations, scenarios, and connections from a recent business unit.

title: Error Analysis Clinic
summary: Students diagnose incorrect answers, calculations, claims, or business decisions, explain the source of each error, and produce a stronger corrected response.
scenario: Students analyze fictional responses from a business assessment that include vocabulary confusion, unsupported claims, calculation errors, and weak recommendations.

title: Concept Sort Challenge
summary: Students sort terms, examples, scenarios, evidence, or decisions into meaningful categories, defend their placements, and revise the structure when competing interpretations appear.
scenario: Students sort business examples into categories such as fixed or variable cost, need or want, primary or secondary research, and ethical or unethical practice.

title: Assessment Corrections Conference
summary: Students prepare evidence-based corrections, explain their original thinking, meet briefly with the teacher or a peer reviewer, and earn credit through demonstrated understanding rather than answer replacement.
scenario: Students revise selected questions from a recent business assessment and defend each correction during a short structured conference.

title: Mastery Stations
summary: Students rotate through targeted review stations that assess distinct standards, provide immediate feedback, and direct each learner toward the practice needed for mastery.
scenario: Students rotate through stations on vocabulary application, scenario analysis, calculation, evidence use, and written recommendation before a unit assessment.

title: Student-Written Assessment Lab
summary: Students create, critique, revise, and answer assessment questions aligned to course standards, strengthening content knowledge and understanding of what quality evidence of learning looks like.
scenario: Student teams design a mini-assessment for a completed business unit, exchange it with another team, analyze the results, and revise weak questions.
